name: 50 Cent Army
description: The "50 Cent Army" (五毛党, wǔ máo dǎng) is a term used to describe individuals allegedly hired by the Chinese government to post comments online that promote pro-government viewpoints and shape public opinion. The name originates from the alleged payment of 50 Chinese cents (毛, máo) per post. The theory posits that this army is a coordinated effort to flood online forums, social media platforms, and comment sections with propaganda, deflecting criticism and spreading disinformation. Proponents cite leaked documents, anecdotal accounts, and analysis of online posting patterns as evidence. They point to the sheer volume of pro-government comments, often simplistic and repetitive, appearing shortly after sensitive events or critical news reports. Critics, including the Chinese government, deny the existence of a centrally organized and paid "army." They argue that many pro-government comments are genuine expressions of support from patriotic citizens. Academics researching online discourse acknowledge the presence of coordinated pro-government messaging but debate the scale, organization, and effectiveness of such efforts. Some argue that the 50 Cent Army is more of a decentralized phenomenon, involving both paid individuals and volunteers motivated by nationalism or political alignment. Furthermore, some scholars suggest that the term is often used hyperbolically to discredit any opinion that aligns with the Chinese government, regardless of its origin.
subtitle: Alleged Chinese Government Online Propaganda Force

claims.claim: The Chinese government employs a large group of individuals to post pro-government comments online.
claims.evidence: Leaked documents, anecdotal accounts, analysis of posting patterns.
claims.criticism: The Chinese government denies the existence of a centrally organized and paid "army."

claims.claim: These individuals are paid 50 cents (毛, máo) per post.
claims.evidence: Origin of the name, anecdotal evidence.
claims.criticism: Difficult to verify payment amounts, likely varies depending on task and individual.

claims.claim: The 50 Cent Army's primary goal is to shape public opinion in favor of the Chinese government.
claims.evidence: Analysis of content posted, timing of posts relative to sensitive events.
claims.criticism: Determining intent is subjective; pro-government sentiments may stem from genuine support.

keyFigures.name: Xiao Qiang
keyFigures.role: Critic and researcher of Chinese internet censorship and propaganda

keyFigures.name: Guo Wengui
keyFigures.role: Chinese businessman who has made claims about the 50 Cent Army

keyFigures.name: Various Chinese government spokespersons
keyFigures.role: Deny the existence of a centrally organized and paid 50 Cent Army

timeline.event: Emergence of online discussion and the alleged beginnings of pro-government commentary.
Increased attention to online commentators during the Beijing Olympics and subsequent events.
Ongoing debate and research into the scale and impact of the 50 Cent Army and similar online influence operations.

sources.author: Bamberger, J., & ten Oever, N.
sources.work: When the state meets the internet: explaining China’s internet management system.
sources.title: Bamberger, J., & ten Oever, N.

sources.author: King, G. W., Pan, J., & Roberts, M. E.
sources.work: How the Chinese Government Fabricates Social Media Posts for Strategic Distraction, Not Engaged Argument.
sources.title: King, G. W., Pan, J., & Roberts, M. E.
